title: What concrete actions does the Fable unknowns article recommend?
note: User asked for the actionable, tangible practices suggested by the article.
synthesis: The article recommends concrete artifacts and workflows for surfacing unknowns: ask for a blindspot pass, prototype or brainstorm before implementation, have the agent interview you, provide references, review an implementation plan focused on change-prone decisions, keep implementation notes during work, package a pitch/explainer afterward, and quiz yourself before merging.

interaction.content: what are the actionable and tangible things he suggests to do?

The tangible moves are: 1. Ask for a blindspot pass before starting unfamiliar work. 2. Make brainstorms and quick prototypes when you do not know what you want until you see it. 3. Have the agent interview you one question at a time about ambiguous decisions. 4. Give references, especially source code, when words are not enough. 5. Ask for an implementation plan that surfaces tweakable decisions first: data models, interfaces, UX flows. 6. During implementation, keep implementation-notes.md so deviations and edge cases are logged. 7. After implementation, create pitch/explainer artifacts for reviewers. 8. Ask for a quiz or change report before merging so you can prove you understand what changed. The common pattern: turn vague uncertainty into an artifact you can react to, review, or learn from. Check-back: which of those actions happens before implementation, which happens during, and which happens after?

title: What are the four kinds of unknowns in agent work?
note: User paused on the known known / known unknown / unknown known / unknown unknown taxonomy.
synthesis: The four unknown types separate what is already specified, what is consciously unresolved, what is tacit but unstated, and what is outside the user's current frame. In agentic work, failures often come from the last two: the agent cannot infer your unstated taste or domain assumptions, and neither of you may notice a missing constraint until the real task exposes it.

interaction.content:
these things:

Known Knowns: This is essentially what is in my prompt. What do I tell the agent that I want?
Known Unknowns: What haven't I figured out yet, but I’m aware that I haven’t?
Unknown Knowns: What's so obvious I’d never write it down, but would recognize it if I saw it?
Unknown Unknowns: What haven't I considered at all? What knowledge am I not aware of? Do I know how good something can be?

Think of these as four buckets for the gap between your prompt and the real task. Known knowns are the explicit instructions: 'add a login button to the header.' Known unknowns are open decisions you know about: 'I haven't decided whether login opens a modal or goes to a new page.' Unknown knowns are tacit expectations: you never said it, but you would instantly notice if the button looked off-brand or appeared on mobile in the wrong place. Unknown unknowns are missing considerations outside your frame: maybe the app already has auth middleware, accessibility rules, analytics events, or a security constraint you did not know existed. The article's point is that better agent work means surfacing these buckets earlier, before the agent guesses badly. Check-back: for a login button task, give me one example of an unknown known versus an unknown unknown.

title: Is the unknowns examples page a helping thing?
note: User asked whether the linked companion page is the practical helping artifact for the article's map/territory unknowns idea.
synthesis: Yes. The page is the practical companion to the article: it turns the abstract map-versus-territory idea into example HTML artifacts that help discover unknowns before, during, and after implementation. It is a toolkit for making gaps visible early.

interaction.content: Yes. It is the helping thing in a very literal sense: a companion page with example HTML artifacts for discovering unknowns. The article gives the concept: the map is not the territory, and the gap is your unknowns. This page gives practical tools for finding those gaps: blindspot passes, prototypes, interviews, references, implementation notes, pitch docs, and quizzes. So instead of just saying 'clarify your unknowns,' it shows concrete artifacts you can ask an agent to make. Check-back: which feels more useful to you right now: a blindspot pass before work starts, or implementation notes while the work is happening?

title: How does map versus territory explain agent unknowns?
note: User paused on how the map/territory metaphor relates to the article's argument about agentic coding.
synthesis: In the article, the map is the prompt, plan, skill file, or context you give the agent. The territory is the actual task: the codebase, constraints, edge cases, product taste, and real-world mess. Unknowns are the gaps between those two. When the map is incomplete, the agent must guess what the territory requires.

interaction.content: the map is not the territory. how is this related?

The map is what you give the agent: your prompt, plan, examples, skills, and context. The territory is the real thing the agent has to work inside: the actual codebase, the product constraints, the edge cases, your taste, the user need, and all the messy details. The article is saying: agent failures often happen in the gap between those two. Your prompt might sound clear as a map, but the real task may contain things the map did not describe. Those gaps are what Thariq calls unknowns. So when Claude or Fable makes a bad choice, it may not be because it cannot code. It may be because the map you gave it did not match the territory it had to navigate. Check-back: imagine you ask an agent to add a login button. What is one thing that might exist in the territory that was not obvious from that simple map?

title: How should an audio tutor walk me through the Fable unknowns article?
note: Prompt for another agent to read the article in logical sections, nearly verbatim, while adding simple context and checking readiness before moving on.
synthesis: Use this prompt to run a spoken guided reading of the article. The tutor should read each section almost verbatim, add brief context in plain language, ask one understanding check, then wait until the learner says they are ready to continue.

interaction.content:
i want you to break this article up into logical sections, add more context/colour to it, etc

create me a prompt with those sections, so another agent will just talk me through each section in plain and simple terms, engaging me, asking me if ready to move to next - i will be speaking via audio

JUST ENSURE EVERY SINGLE SECTION IS READ TO ME ALMOST VERBATIME

Created a guided audio-reading prompt that splits the article into logical sections. Each section includes a near-verbatim passage, a short plain-English context note, and a check-back before continuing.
